title: Rally / Jira / ADO — Work-Item Scope Storage Comparison
topic: Scope storage and move semantics in Rally, Jira, ADO — validate Vector PLA-0043
summary: How three competitor PM tools store work-item scope, compute subtree queries, and handle moves — used to lock Vector PLA-0043 schema.
content:
Summary

All three products converge on a single-owner model: a work item belongs to exactly one container at creation time (Rally Project, Jira Project, ADO Area Path), with no native multi-project membership. Hierarchy storage diverges meaningfully — Rally uses a denormalised ancestry array (_ProjectHierarchy) in its Lookback API enabling single-clause subtree queries, ADO exposes a materialised-path string in its Classification Nodes API making subtree queries prefix-matches, while Jira has no project hierarchy at all and uses a flat project model. Move semantics also diverge: ADO orphans children cross-project (links survive but children stay), Jira requires children to be moved separately (not automatic), and Rally has no documented atomic cross-project move — hierarchies require manual re-parenting via WSAPI. Permission inheritance follows the storage pattern: ADO inherits downward by default (can be disabled per node), Rally requires per-project explicit grants with no documented cascade, and Jira uses per-project permission schemes with no cross-project inheritance.

Comparison Table

Product Q1 — Work-item ownership Q2 — Hierarchy storage Q3 — Move semantics Q4 — Permission inheritance
Rally Single project FK per artifact [1][2] Adjacency list in WSAPI (Project + projectScopeDown); Lookback API adds denormalised _ProjectHierarchy array enabling single-clause subtree queries [2][3] No atomic cross-project move; hierarchies must be re-parented individually via WSAPI; child tasks not automatically moved [4] No documented cascade; per-project explicit grants required [5]
Jira Single project FK per issue; flat project model — no sub-projects [6][7] No project hierarchy; projects are independent and flat [7] Subtasks do NOT move automatically with parent; must be bulk-moved separately; status mapping required [8][9] No cross-project inheritance; each project has its own permission scheme [10]
Azure DevOps Single Area Path (Classification Node) per work item; exactly one Area Path field value per item [11] Materialised-path string stored in path field (e.g. \Fabrikam Fiber\Area\Service Delivery\Voice); subtree queries use path-prefix matching or CLI --depth traversal [12][13] Children do NOT move with parent across projects; links are preserved but children remain in origin project [14] Area path permissions inherit DOWN by default; can be disabled per node via Inheritance slider; explicit child overrides take precedence [15][16]

Detailed Findings

Rally

Q1 — Ownership: The Rally WSAPI documentation explicitly states: "All artifacts are scoped to a project" and "The Project attribute can be assigned to any single project in the workspace." [2] Every User Story, Defect, Task, Iteration, and Release object carries a single Project foreign key. Multi-project membership for a single artifact is not supported and not documented.

Q2 — Hierarchy storage: At the WSAPI layer, each Project node stores a reference to its parent project (adjacency list). Queries scope down by passing projectScopeDown=true to walk the tree, or scope up with projectScopeUp=true. [1][2] The Lookback API (analytics layer) goes further: it stores a denormalised _ProjectHierarchy field on each artifact — an ordered array of all ancestor project OIDs from root down to the item's direct project. A single query clause like { "_ProjectHierarchy": 7890 } returns all items anywhere in that subtree without recursive joins. [3] This is a hybrid: adjacency list at the project-node level, denormalised ancestry at the artifact level for efficient scope-down reads.

Q3 — Move semantics: There is no atomic "move artifact to project" operation documented in primary sources. The Broadcom knowledge base states explicitly: "There is no direct, automated means for moving projects or artifacts to another workspace" and "There is not an automated way to move entire hierarchies." [4] Re-parenting individual artifacts via the WSAPI requires updating the Project field on each item separately. Child tasks are not automatically moved — the documentation notes tasks must be re-associated manually. Note: the KB article addresses cross-workspace moves; same-workspace project-field updates via WSAPI are technically possible but cascade behaviour is undocumented (see Gaps).

Q4 — Permissions: Rally permissions are set per user per project (Viewer / Editor / Project Admin). [5] The UI displays each project node individually. The Broadcom TechDocs do not document an automatic cascade from parent to child projects, and no primary-source evidence of a propagate-to-children feature was found. This is flagged as a gap.

Jira

Q1 — Ownership: Jira uses a flat project model — there are no sub-projects. Each issue belongs to exactly one project via a single FK. The project field on an issue is a single reference. Sharing an issue across multiple projects is not supported; the only workaround is linking. [6][7]

Q2 — Hierarchy storage: Because Jira has no project hierarchy, this question is moot for the project dimension. Projects are peer entities. Issue hierarchy (Epic > Story > Sub-task) is stored via parent link fields within a single project, not across a project tree. [7] Jira does not have an area-path-style tree or recursive project scoping.

Q3 — Move semantics: Moving an issue to another project requires an explicit Move action. Sub-tasks are not automatically moved with their parent — the Atlassian support page states: "If your work items have subtasks, then you will need to move the subtasks to the destination space as well." [8] A community-confirmed bug report (JRACLOUD-71354) describes errors when moving parents that have sub-tasks in inconsistent states. [9] Cross-project links are preserved post-move. Status mapping is required because destination projects may use different workflows.

Q4 — Permissions: Jira uses permission schemes assigned per project. There is no automatic inheritance between projects because there is no project hierarchy. Each project is governed by its own permission scheme. Shared schemes can apply the same rules to multiple projects, but changes to a shared scheme affect all those projects simultaneously — this is lateral sharing, not hierarchical inheritance. [10]

Azure DevOps

Q1 — Ownership: Each work item has a single Area field that holds one Area Path value. A work item can appear on multiple team backlogs (because a team can subscribe to multiple area paths with includeSubAreas=true), but the work item itself carries only one Area Path value at a time. Multi-area-path assignment at the work-item level is not supported. [11]

Q2 — Hierarchy storage: Area Paths (Classification Nodes) are stored with an explicit materialised path string. The REST API response for a node includes a path field such as \Fabrikam Fiber\Area\Service Delivery\Voice. [12] The Azure CLI az boards area project list --depth 3 output shows full path strings for each row, consistent with server-side path-prefix traversal. There is also a parent link in the API response pointing to the parent node URL, so both a stored path string and a parent reference co-exist. [13] Microsoft's public documentation does not name the internal DB pattern explicitly, but the API shape — materialised path string + parent link + depth parameter — is consistent with a materialised-path storage model backed by an adjacency-list parent reference for mutation operations.

Q3 — Move semantics: Microsoft's official documentation explicitly states: "Child work items don't get moved and remain in the origin project, but the Parent-Child links remain in place." [14] Moving a parent work item to a different project orphans its children in the origin project (they retain the link but are now cross-project). Area Path reassignment within a project (e.g. /Eng/Backend to /Eng/Frontend) does not carry this restriction — items can be bulk-updated; each item's Area Path field is updated individually.

Q4 — Permissions: ADO permissions on area path nodes inherit downward by default. The official documentation states: "Permissions set at a higher-level node get inherited by all subnodes unless explicitly overridden." [15] Administrators can disable this via the Inheritance slider on the Security dialog for any node, which converts all inherited permissions to explicit ACEs. Explicit child-level overrides take precedence over parent-inherited permissions. [16]

What This Means for Vector PLA-0043

All three competitors agree on the single-FK ownership model: a work item belongs to exactly one container node, and scope queries filter to "this node and descendants." That validates Vector's current design. For subtree query performance, the most relevant precedent is ADO's materialised path — storing the full ancestry path string on each org_node row would make scope-down queries a cheap LIKE '\Eng\%' prefix scan rather than a recursive CTE walk on every request. Rally's Lookback API took the same idea further by denormalising the ancestry onto the artifact row itself. Vector's current adjacency list on org_nodes is correct for mutation (add/move/archive a node) but should add a materialised-path column for the read-path scope queries to match the industry pattern. On move semantics, all three products leave children behind when a work item crosses project/container boundaries (no automatic cascade), so Vector does not need a cascading-move constraint — but it does need a UI warning when children would be orphaned. On RBAC, ADO's downward-inheriting permission model is the only one with a built-in cascade and aligns well with Vector's topology tree; Jira and Rally both require per-node explicit grants which is a UX friction point Vector can improve on by making inheritance the default and per-node override the exception.

Gaps and Limitations

  • Rally — internal DB storage model: The Rally WSAPI documentation describes the API shape (single Project FK, projectScopeDown parameter) but does not disclose the internal database storage mechanism. The adjacency-list characterisation at the project-node level is inferred from the API (each artifact has one Project reference; projectScopeDown implies walking children), not from schema documentation. Broadcom has not published internal DB schemas.
  • Rally — permission cascade: No primary-source documentation was found confirming or denying automatic permission inheritance from parent to child projects. The editing UI suggests per-node explicit grants are required, but a propagate-to-children feature may exist at workspace-admin level and is not confirmable from available public docs (docs.broadcom.com root returned 404; knowledge.broadcom.com requires login for many articles).
  • Rally — move semantics within same workspace: The Broadcom KB article addresses cross-workspace moves only. Moving an artifact between projects within the same workspace via WSAPI (updating the Project field) is technically possible but documented cascade behaviours for children were not found in primary sources.
  • ADO — exact internal storage schema: Microsoft has not published the TFS/ADO internal SQL schema for Classification Nodes. The materialised-path characterisation is based on the API shape (path string + parent link + depth-based retrieval) and is a strong inference, not a confirmed schema description.
  • Jira — move issue primary doc 404: The direct Atlassian support URL for "move-an-issue-to-another-project" returned a 404. Move semantics were confirmed via the bulk-move support page and community sources.
  • All products — doc currency: Rally/Broadcom TechDocs are undated on several pages. ADO docs are dated 2025–2026. Jira docs are dated 2024–2025. All findings are considered current as of May 2026.
